coredata.dc:title: Copper-doped carbon dots nanozymes for multi-signal specific hydrogen sulfide assay and broad-spectrum antibacterial
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zihan
Zhou, Mingyang
Zhao, Lizhi
Hu, Wei
Wang, Xin
Wei, Hua
Tong, Xinxin
Yao, Changqing
Li, Xiaoqing
Zhang, Yutong
Liu, Jian
Yang, Shenghong
coredata.dc:description: The emergence of carbon dots-based nanozymes (CDNEs) has opened a broad horizon for multi-mode analysis and safety antibacterial application. However, the practical applications of CDNEs are currently hindered by unsatisfactory performance and complicated synthetic methodology. In this study, we developed a novel copper-doped CDNEs (Cu-CDNEs) with both excellent fluorescence property and peroxidase-like activity. The Cu-CDNEs can be synthesized easily by pyrolyzing a solid mixture of copper glutamate and ethylenediamine hydrochloride for 5 min. By investigating the inhibition of H2S on the peroxidase-like activity of Cu-CDNEs and the competitive interaction between H2S and 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine with reactive oxygen species, we established a dual-mode analysis system for colorimetric and fluorescent detection of H2S with the limit of detection of 20 nM and 25 nM, respectively. Furthermore, a convenient scanometric analysis mode also was developed and successfully applied for the accurate determination of H2S in actual water samples with recoveries of 96.8 %–107.7 %. Moreover, the designed Cu-CDNEs show good cytocompatibility from MTT results, and demonstrated broad-spectrum antibacterial capacity against Gram-positive/negative bacteria due to the surface positive charge and peroxidase-like activity. This study may provide valuable insights for rational design and broad applications in environmental and biomedical fields using functional nanozymes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots-based nanozymes
Colorimetric and fluorescent analysis
H2S detection
Broad-spectrum antibacterial

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Colorimetric and fluorescence detection of H2S
Colorimetric and fluorescent detection of H2S
Detection of H2S in actual samples

ZR2022MC153, ZR2023MA089), the Program of New Collegiate 20 Items in Jinan (Nos. 2021GXRC042, 202228031), the Science, Education and Industry Integration Innovation Pilot Project from Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences) (Nos. 2022JBZ02-04, 2024GH25, 2023PY051)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The excitation and emission spectra of Cu-CDNEs (A), the UV–Visible absorption spectra of Cu-CDNEs-TMB/H2O2/TMB-H2O2 (B), the emission spectra of Cu-CDs, N-CDs and Cu-CDNEs (C), the UV–Visible absorption spectra of Glu-Cu/Cu-CDs/N-CDs/Cu-CDNEs-TMB-H2O2 (D).

The TEM images of Cu-CDNEs (A), inset: Size distribution of Cu-CDNEs. The high-resolution TEM image and HAADF-STEM image (B) and XRD pattern of Cu-CDNEs (C). FTIR spectra of Cu-CDNEs, Cu-CDs, N-CDs. (D). High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (E), N 1s (F), O 1s (G) and Cu 2p (H) of Cu-CDNEs, Cu-CDs, and N-CDs/Glu-Cu.

The UV–Visible absorption spectra of Cu-CDNEs, OPD-Cu-CDNEs, OPD-H2O2, and OPD-Cu-CDNEs-H2O2 (A). The free radical scavenging experiment by using TBA, PBQ and Try as trapping agents (B). The EPR spectra of DMPO-⋅OH and O2⋅−, TEMP-1O2 for Cu-CDNEs-H2O2 (C). The relative peroxidase-like activity of Cu-CDNEs at different pH (D). The steady-state kinetic assay of Cu-CDNEs-catalyzed oxidation of TMB by H2O2 (E, F). The steady-state kinetic assay of Cu-CDNEs-catalyzed decomposition of H2O2 (G, H).

The UV–Visible absorption (A) and fluorescence (D) spectra of Cu-CDNEs and Cu-CDNEs-TMB-H2O2 before and after S2− added. The UV–Visible absorption (B) and fluorescence (E) spectra of Cu-CDNEs and Cu-CDNEs-TMB-H2O2 with different concentrations S2− added. Plots of A0-A (C) and I/I0 (F) with the different concentrations of S2− based on colorimetric and fluorescent mode, respectively.

Effect of different HAc-NaAc buffer concentrations (A) and pH (B) on the signal response of H2S and biothiols. Two-dimensional PCA plot of the detection system for 0.5 μM (C), 1 μM (D), 3 μM (E), 5 μM (F) of H2S and biothiols.

Agar plate digital photographs of bacterial colonies of E. coli (A1) and S. aureus (B1) with different treatments. Antibacterial effects on E. coil (A2) and S. aureus (B2) of Cu-CDNEs at different concentrations. Antibacterial effects on E. coil (A3) and S. aureus (B3) of Cu-CDNEs-H2O2 at different concentration. Corresponding bacterial viability measurement of E. coil (A4) and S. aureus (B4) with different treatments. Corresponding bacterial viability measurement of E. coil (A5) and S. aureus (B5) with of Cu-CDNEs and Cu-CDNEs-H2O2 at different concentration.
Schematic diagram of designed Cu-CDNEs for colorimetric and fluorescent detection of H2S and broad-spectrum antibacterial.
Scanometric analysis mode for detecting H2S in real water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Copper-doped carbon dots nanozymes for multi-signal specific hydrogen sulfide assay and broad-spectrum antibacterial

Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanxi Institute of Energy, Jinzhong, Shanxi 030600, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The emergence of carbon dots-based nanozymes (CDNEs) has opened a broad horizon for multi-mode analysis and safety antibacterial application. However, the practical applications of CDNEs are currently hindered by unsatisfactory performance and complicated synthetic methodology. In this study, we developed a novel copper-doped CDNEs (Cu-CDNEs) with both excellent fluorescence property and peroxidase-like activity. The Cu-CDNEs can be synthesized easily by pyrolyzing a solid mixture of copper glutamate and ethylenediamine hydrochloride for 5 min. By investigating the inhibition of H2S on the peroxidase-like activity of Cu-CDNEs and the competitive interaction between H2S and 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine with reactive oxygen species, we established a dual-mode analysis system for colorimetric and fluorescent detection of H2S with the limit of detection of 20 nM and 25 nM, respectively. Furthermore, a convenient scanometric analysis mode also was developed and successfully applied for the accurate determination of H2S in actual water samples with recoveries of 96.8 %–107.7 %. Moreover, the designed Cu-CDNEs show good cytocompatibility from MTT results, and demonstrated broad-spectrum antibacterial capacity against Gram-positive/negative bacteria due to the surface positive charge and peroxidase-like activity. This study may provide valuable insights for rational design and broad applications in environmental and biomedical fields using functional nanozymes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Sulfur serves as a crucial role in the physiological functions of many bioactive species, including methionine, biothiols compounds, and hydrogen sulfide (H2S) [1–3]. Notably, H2S has been acknowledged as the third important gaseous transmitter that regulates physiological systems [4], but abnormal levels of H2S in vivo can exert great threaten to health, leading diabetes, cirrhosis, Alzheimer's disease, Down syndrome, etc [5,6]. The growing sulfide industry creates more possibilities to expose humans to H2S, and H2S also was listed as one of most dangerous gases that harming human environment by the US Occupational Safety and Health Administration [7–10]. Thus, there is an urgent need for accurate and sensitive analysis of H2S for environmental monitoring purpose as well as ensuring food safety and disease diagnosis.

Although conventional detection methods, such as high-performance liquid chromatography [11], inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectrometry [12] and electrochemical method [13], have achieved accurate determination of H2S, they are still limited by expensive instruments, the need for well-trained operators, and time-consuming, which hinder real-time and on-the-spot analysis [14]. In contrast, colorimetric and fluorescent methods offer significant advantages in field detection of analytes as they provide direct results through visual observation or UV scanning while minimizing reliance on skilled operators and complicated equipments [15–17]. Unfortunately, the spectroscopic detection of H2S is often affected by interference from biothiols [18], and the development of a dual-mode analysis system for H2S remains unsatisfactory [19–23]. Therefore, it is still a pressing demand to design functional materials for constructing robust, rapid-response, and high-specificity spectral analysis platforms to address the current challenges in H2S detection.

The anti-oxidative stress effect of H2S provides a broad-new perspective for addressing bacterial antibiotic resistance and tolerance by using antibiotic to inhibit the H2S biogenesis [24,25]. However, the excessive reliance on antibiotics in the field of antibacterial research poses a significant burden on the ecological balance, thereby seriously threatening the human living environment [26–28]. Currently, sterilization through reactive oxygen species (ROS) is highly advocated as an alternative strategy to mitigate antibiotic overuse, thus the materials with enzyme-mimic activity that can catalyze O2/H2O2 to generate ROS are highly desired [29–31]. Furthermore, materials with small size and positively charged surfaces are expected to enhance their binding affinity toward bacteria and achieve superior antibacterial efficiency [32,33]. Hence, exploring advanced materials to combat bacterial infection is also imperative.

Carbon dots-based nanozymes (CDNEs) represent a novel combination of carbon dots (CDs) and traditional nanozymes, integrating the optical properties of CDs with the enzyme-like activities of nanozymes. This unique hybrid material holds great potential for applications in biological and chemical analysis [34–36], contaminant degradation [37], and antibacterial, etc [38,39]. Specially, CDNEs exhibit lower cell toxicity compared to traditional heterogeneous metal or metal oxide particles nanozymes, making them promising candidates for biomedical applications [40,41]. However, achieving both favorable fluorescence performance and useable enzyme-like activities in existing CDNEs remains challenging, which limits their use in multi-mode analysis system [23]. In addition, large-scale application of CDNEs in antibacterial fields was hindered by harsh synthesis routes that require excessive energy/time/solvent consumption and high-cost [42–44]. Therefore, it is crucial to develop new protocols for synthesizing CDNEs with excellent fluorescence performance and enzyme-like activity for realizing accurate detection of H2S as well as broad-spectrum antibacterial activity.

The potential advantages of CDNEs in multi-mode analysis and antibacterial application sparked us to improve the performance and synthetic methodology of that for reasonable applications. In this study, a high-efficiency solvent-free strategy was proposed for synthesizing copper-doped CDNEs (Cu-CDNEs) by simple pyrolyzing a solid mixture of cupric glutamate (Glu-Cu) and ethylenediamine hydrochloride (EDA·2HCl) for 5 min (Scheme 1 ). The involvement of EDA·2HCl imparts excellent blue fluorescence with a quantum yield of 31.2 % to Cu-CDNEs, along with peroxidase-like activity that enables the catalysis of H2O2 to generate ROS. Leveraging the versatility of Cu-CDNEs, we successfully contracted a highly sensitive and selective colorimetric and fluorescence dual-mode detection platform for H2S, which was further applied for the determination of H2S in real samples. Moreover, the designed Cu-CDNEs also exhibit broad-spectrum antibacterial capacity against Gram-negative/positive bacterium due to the synergistic effect between their autologous surface positive charge and ROS production from catalyzing H2O2, making them promising candidates as antibacterial materials. We anticipate that this research will provide valuable insights into facilitating the facile preparation and extensive applications of CDNEs.

L-Glutamic acid (Glu), ethylenediamine dihydrochloride (EDA·2HCl), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), tert-butyl alcohol (TBA), p-benzoquinone (PBQ), sodium sulfide (Na2S), tryptophan (Try) were purchased from Aladdin Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 %) and copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5H2O) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the reagents were used directly without extra purification process and ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.25 MΩ·cm was used during the whole experiment.

Typically, 0.1 g of cupric glutamate (Glu-Cu) and 0.2 g of ethylenediamine dihydrochloride (EDA·2HCl) were finely ground into homogeneous powders in an agate mortar and transferred to a 50 mL beaker. Subsequently, the mixture was heated for 5 min in a domestic microwave oven (800 W). The crude products were dispersed in dimethyl sulfoxide, and the supinate was collected and dialyzed (molecular weight cut-off 500) for 24 h. Then the products were freeze-dried for further application, designated as Cu-CDNEs. As controls, Cu-CDs and N-CDs were synthesized through similar procedures but with Glu-Cu and EDA·2HCl as raw materials, respectively.

The peroxidase-like activity of Cu-CDNEs was evaluated by using TMB-H2O2 as substrate. Typically, at room temperature, 100 μL of TMB (10 mM), 100 μL of Cu-CDNEs dispersion solution (1.0 mg⋅mL−1), and 100 μL of H2O2 (1.0 M) were added to 2.7 mL HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH=4, 0.1 M) in sequence. After reacted for 5 min, the absorbances at 652 nm were recorded. The kinetic assays were described in Supporting Information in detail.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and fluorescence detection of H2S
ce:section.ce:para: For colorimetric detection, 100 μL Cu-CDNEs (1.0 mg⋅mL−1), 100 μL of TMB (10 mM), 30 μL different concentrations Na2S solutions, and 100 μL of H2O2 solution (1.0 M) were added to 2.67 mL HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH=4.0, 0.1 M) in turn. After incubation for 5 min, the absorbances of 652 nm were measured to draw standard curve. The chromogenic images were taken by a self-build portable device as described in our previous work [20] and related RGB values were read by Image J. for fabricating scanometric analysis mode and performing principal component analysis (PCA). For fluorescent detection, the operate procedures are like colorimetric analysis, after reacted for 5 min, the photoluminescence (PL) intensities at 421 nm (excitation wavelength: 360 nm) were recorded to establish standard curve. The specificity of proposed methods was checked by using biothiols compounds, common amino acids and ions as interfering substances through similar procedure to H2S, including cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcy), and glutathione (GSH), Glutamic acid (Glu), Serine (Ser), SO4 2−, H2PO4 −, CO3 2−, Cl−, F−, Ac−, HPO4 2−, Ca2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Zn2+, Ba2+.

The Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) was cultured on the Luria-Bertani (LB) liquid medium at 37 ℃ for 8 h to reach the logarithmic growth period. The plate counting method was used to assess the antibacterial performance of Cu-CDNEs. The experiments were accomplished in HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH = 4.5). Typically, the antibacterial experiments were divided to four groups: (1) bacteria, (2) bacteria + H2O2, (3) bacteria + Cu-CDNEs, (4) bacteria + Cu-CDNEs + H2O2, and each group contained the same number of bacteria. The mixtures were incubated in a 37 ℃ shaker with a shaking speed of 200 rpm for 40 min. Subsequently, 100 μL of the diluted mixtures were coated on agar plates and cultured at 37 ℃ for 18 h. Finally, the relative bacterial activity was obtained from counting the numbers of colonies.

The Cu-CDNEs with both high fluorescence and enzyme-like activity was synthesized through a simple microwave-assisted solid-phase synthesis strategy that pyrolyzing the mixture of Glu-Cu and EDA·2HCl for 5 min. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the synthesized Cu-CDNEs can emit bright blue fluorescence with an emission center of 421 nm under the optimal excitation wavelength of 360 nm, and the quantum yield can reach 31.2 % (quinine sulfate as reference). However, the Cu-CDs and N-CDs, obtained by pyrolyzing the Glu-Cu and EDA·2HCl, respectively, just exhibit ignorable fluorescence performance (Fig. 1C). The better fluorescence of Cu-CDNEs should be attributed to the reasonable surface passivation and nitrogen doping effect of EDA·2HCl, which inhibits the non-radiative recombination and further promotes the radiative recombination meanwhile [45]. The fluorescence spectra of Cu-CDNEs also show the excitation-dependent characteristic in Fig. S1, the emission wavelengths of Cu-CDNEs occur significant red-shift as the excitation wavelengths increase from 340 nm to 460 nm. And the typical absorption of Cu-CDNEs at 370 nm can be assigned to the n-π* transitions (Fig. S2) [46]. The Cu-CDNEs also exhibits well peroxidase-like activity, which was preliminarily verified with TMB as substrate. As shown in Fig. 1B, the TMB can be oxidized to oxTMB with the solution color changes from colorless to blue in the presence of Cu-CDNEs and H2O2. What’s more, both Cu-Glu and N-CDs cannot show obvious peroxidase-like activity except the Cu-CDs with faint peroxidase-like activity (Fig. 1D). Above results indicate that the excellent fluorescent performance and peroxidase-like activity of Cu-CDNEs should be attributed to the synergistic reaction effect of Cu-Glu and EDA·2HCl. Besides, the reaction conditions of Cu-CDNEs also were optimized systemically, and the Cu-CDNEs synthesized by the mass ratio of Glu-Cu and EDA·2HCl at 1:2 for reacting 5 min exhibits relatively high PL performance and enzyme-like activity (Fig. S3), which lay a good foundation for further applications.

As shown in TEM images, The Cu-CDNEs exhibit a distinct crystal structure with lattice fringes spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite (Fig. 2 A and B) and the small bright spots (marked with yellow circles) from high angle annular dark field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) images indicate Cu atoms have been successfully doped in Cu-CDNEs (Fig. 2B) [23]. The Cu-CDNEs is mono-dispersed with size distribution of 2–5 nm, and the average size is about 3.4 nm (Fig. 2A inset). The XRD pattern in Fig. 2C shows the Cu-CDNEs possess a graphite-like structure due the presence of wide diffraction peak at about 25° [45], and the typical peaks of Cu metals cannot be observed, which indicates that Cu atoms are dispersed on the carbon dots [23]. The FT-IR and XPS spectra were measured to analyze the surface structure of Cu-CDNEs. In FT-IR spectrum, the characteristic absorptions of ν(OH), ν(NH), ν(CH), ν(CO), ν(CN) and ν(CO) can be found at 3551 cm−1, 3471 cm−1, 3409 cm−1, 1639 cm−1, 1615 cm−1 and 1506 cm−1, respectively, which confirmed that Cu-CDNEs are rich in oxygen and nitrogen functional groups (Fig. 2D). In addition, a NCuN peak appeared at 1021 cm−1, which proved that the Cu atom has been doped to the Cu-CDNEs successfully [47,48].

The XPS was applied to analyze the surface structure and valence state of CDNEs. The full XPS spectrum suggests the existence of C, N, O and Cu elements (Fig. S4). The high-resolution C 1s spectrum indicates the existence of CO/CN (288.1 eV), CO/CN (285.9 eV), and CC/CC (284.8 eV) (Fig. 2E). The N 1s band can be deconvoluted into four peaks at 401.2 eV, 399.8 eV, 399.0 eV and 399.3 eV, representing Graphitic-N, Pyrrolic-N, CuN and Pyridinic-N, respectively [23] (Fig. 2F). In the O 1s high-resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 2G), two typical peaks can be observed at 531.3 eV (CO) and 532.5 eV (CO). As show in Fig. 2H, the high-resolution Cu 2p spectra show that the peaks of 953.8 eV and 933.7 eV can be assigned to Cu (II) species and the peaks at 951.5 and 931.6 eV were attributed to Cu (I) species, revealing that partial Cu2+ species were in situ reduced during microwave pyrolysis [8]. The content of CuN in Cu-CDs and Cu-CDNEs was improved from 23.5 % to 30.1 %, and the Cu-CDNEs can realize rapider electron transfer and exhibit higher catalytic activity [23]. Notably, the Cu (I) species content in CDNEs (45.1 %) is much higher than that in Cu-CDs (19.6 %) and Glu-Cu (0 %), indicating the high catalytic activity of CDNEs also partly originated from the existence of mixed-valence Cu(I/II) species with a suitable ratio [49]. Therefore, the excellent catalytic activity of Cu-CDNEs should be ascribed to the increasement of Cu-N contents and suitable mixed-valence Cu (I/II) species amounts.

It is generally believed that the oxidation of TMB to oxTMB is caused by reactive oxygen species (ROS) produced in the catalytic system. Then o-phenylenediamine (OPD) was used as substrate to further verify this hypothesis. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the colorless OPD can be oxidized to yellow 2,3-diaminophenazine with a typical absorption at 450 nm by ROS produced from the system of Cu-CDNEs-H2O2. Furthermore, tert-butanol (TBA), tryptophan (Try) and p-benzoquinone (PBQ) were used as the scavengers of ⋅OH, 1O2and O2⋅−, respectively, to further prove the generation of ROS. As can be seen from Fig. 3B, in the presence of TBA/PBQ/Try, the absorbances of oxTMB at 652 nm significantly decrease with different degrees of color change, indicating that ⋅OH, O2⋅− and 1O2 are generated in the catalysis system of Cu-CDNEs-H2O2. Notably, O2⋅− seems to be the dominant ROS. Besides, strong characteristic EPR signals of DMPO-⋅OH (and DMPO-O2⋅−) and TEMP-1O2 also confirmed the presence of three ROS (Fig. 3C). Generally, most reported nanozymes can exhibit good catalytic activity only at weak acid environment, as a typical pH of 4.0 [50]. Specially, the catalytic activity of developed Cu-CDNEs still can maintain in a broad pH range of 2.0–9.0 (Fig. S5). From Fig. 3D we can see that the catalytic activity of Cu-CDNEs at neutral condition can reach 50 % of that at pH=4, which possesses greater advantages over other reported nanozymes in practical applications [23,51,52].

The catalytic reaction conditions of Cu-CDNEs were optimized for further performing steady-state kinetic assay. The optimal conditions were preferred to 40 μg mL−1 Cu-CDNEs, 30 mM H2O2, 0.3 mM TMB, and 0.1 M HAc-NaAc buffer solution with pH=4 (Fig. S6). The steady-state kinetic measurements were carried out to calculate Michaelis constants (K m) and V max in the catalytic system using Cu-CDNEs as catalyst and TMB/H2O2 as substrates. For TMB, the K m and V max in the catalytic system were 0.076 mM and 4.5 × 10−8 M s−1, respectively (Fig. 3E and F). Furthermore, in Fig. 3G and H, the K m and V max were further calculated to 4.40 mM and 0.85 × 10−9 M s−1 using H2O2 as the substrate. As shown in Table S1, the proposed Cu-CDNEs exhibits higher substrate affinity and reaction rate compared with previous reports, indicating the Cu-CDNEs have perfect peroxidase-like activity that can meet a variety of applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and fluorescent detection of H2S
ce:section.ce:para: To realize the reasonable analysis application, the fluorescence and catalytic stabilities of Cu-CDNEs were assessed carefully. Firstly, the Cu-CDNEs can be stored for more than twelve weeks without significant fluorescence degradation, and the Cu-CDNEs also shows good tolerance to photobleaching, high-salt environment and temperature change (Fig. S7). Furthermore, the catalytic activity of Cu-CDNEs also shows high-stability and long-term duration. The peroxidase-like activity of Cu-CDNEs still can keep 80 % after storing more than two months or suffering hyperthermia treatment (Fig. S8). Above results fully proves that the Cu-CDNEs has enough stability for further applications.

The designed Cu-CDNEs can be as effective sensor for constructing colorimetric and fluorescent detection method of H2S. In Fig. 4 A, the TMB can be oxidized to oxTMB with a typical absorption of 652 nm by the ROS produced from the system of Cu-CDNEs-H2O2. However, in the presence of S2−, the absorbance at 652 nm decreases sharply and the solution color turned to almost colorless. With the increasement of S2− concentrations, the absorbances exhibit a gradient descent in the range of 0.1–100 μM (Fig. 4B and C). The coefficient of determination (R2) is 0.9971 and the limit of detection can reach 20 nM. Based on the visible color change, a convenient scanometric analysis mode was further developed for rapid quantitative analysis. The images were taken using a Smartphone and the RGB values were readout by Image J. In Fig. S9, the (R+G)/B values have a good linear relationship with the concentration of S2− in the range of 0.1–80 μM (R2 = 0.9982), and the LOD is about 30 nM. Like colorimetric mode, the fluorescence analysis method also was developed logically. As shown in Fig. 4D, the addition of TMB or H2O2 cannot lead significant change of the fluorescence intensity of Cu-CDNEs aqueous solution, while the fluorescence of Cu-CDNEs shows apparent quenching effect in simultaneous existence of TMB and H2O2. Interestingly, the S2− can inhibit the process effectively, and the fluorescence intensity gradually increased with the increasement of S2− concentrations (Fig. 4E). In Fig. 4F, a good linear relationship in the range of 0.1–100 μM (R2 = 0.9975) was obtained with the LOD of 25 nM. Furthermore, the analytical performances of proposed methods are better or comparable than that of the previously reported methods for H2S, as shown in Table S2.

The detection mechanism also was speculated from multi-aspects based on series of experiments and research theories. Firstly, the unique interaction of H2S and Cu-CDNEs may be the important factor in this catalytic system. In our previous work, the H2S can react more readily with Cu2+/Cu+ to form stable CuS/Cu2S species and destroy the original activity sites of copper complexes [8]. Therefore, irreversible binding of H2S to Cu species results in the catalytic activity degradation of Cu-CDNEs. Furthermore, the H2S can compete ROS with TMB lead to the decreasement of generated oxTMB amounts. Besides, from the Fig. S10 we can see that the H2S possesses higher reducibility under acidic environment than that under neutral condition, which can reduce blue oxTMB to colorless TMB effectively. Therefore, the involvement of H2S can decrease the signal of oxTMB at 652 nm based on the interaction of Cu-CDNEs and H2S, the competition effect of H2S and TMB to ROS, and the reducing action of H2S to oxTMB in an acidic environment. For fluorescent detection, the absorption of oxTMB at 370 nm substantially overlaps with the excitation peak of Cu-CDNEs, resulting obvious fluorescence decrease of Cu-CDNEs due to the internal filter effect. However, the involvement of H2S can inhibit the formation of oxTMB, and impeding the internal filter effect, which exhibits an efficient fluorescence enhancement of H2S to Cu-CDNEs-TMB-H2O2 (Fig. S11). Consequently, the proposed colorimetric and fluorescent analysis system renders highly potential application for H2S analysis.

The potential interference from biothiols is a key factor in determining the availability of constructed analytical methods, including cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcy), and glutathione (GSH) [53–55]. In this article, the effect of biothiols to H2S detection was research in detail and solved well. It can be seen from Fig. 5 A and B, the selectivity of proposed method shows high-tolerance to buffer concentration and pH fluctuation, and 0.1 M with pH=4 HAc-NaAc buffer was selected for H2S detection in view of the demand of sensitivity (Fig. S12). To further check the anti-interference capability of fabricated sensing system, the principal component analysis (PCA) based on scanometric mode was performed under different H2S concentrations. As shown in Fig. 5C–F, the PCA distribution indicates that the H2S clearly separates from biothiols even at lower concentrations of 0.5 μM, 1.0 μM, 3.0 μM and 5.0 μM. According to the RGB values, corresponding heat maps also were drawn that can be found in Fig. S13A-D, which indicates a significant response gap of H2S and three biothiols under same conditions. The H2S triggered the severe catalytic activity degradation of Cu-CDNEs through generating of CuS/Cu2S species should be responsible for the distinctive specificity of proposed method to H2S. Therefore, the constructed analysis method possesses an excellent capacity to distinguish H2S and three biothiols, enabling a robust practical application.

To further validate the selectivity of developed analysis method to recognize H2S, the effect of common cations and anions also was tested. As shown in Fig. S14, only H2S can cause distinct absorbance decrease and fluorescence enhancement along with a visual color change, while other substances all cannot lead considerable signal response, proving the established detection system has high-selectivity and ensuring the accurate determination of H2S in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of H2S in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: The scanometric mode was firstly applied for the ingenious and rapid detection of H2S in several real water samples, including tap water, drinking water, lake water, and soda water. The chromogenic images were taken using a Smartphone and related RGB values were readout by Image J. As shown in Table 1 , the recoveries ranged from 96.8 % to 107.7 % and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were lower than 6.0 % with spiked concentrations of 5 μM, 30 μM and 70 μM, proving the superiority of constructed assay platform in field detection. Moreover, spiked experiments were also performed to verify the practicality of colorimetric and fluorescent method. As shown in Table S3 and Table S4, the recoveries are 94.0 %–106.3 % and 97.2 %–104.3 % for colorimetric and fluorescent method, respectively, and the RSDs are all less than 5.0 %. The results suggest that the fabricated analysis platform is reliable for the detection of H2S in actual water samples.

The increasing bacterial antibiotic resistance inspires researchers to design alternative materials to solve bacterial infection problems, such as metal–organic frameworks, metal oxides or metal sulfides, and carbon-based nanomaterials [56–58]. The antibacterial strategy of nanomaterials reflects in two aspects: destroying the cell membrane through electrostatic attraction and exerting toxic actions on bacteria by ROS [33,59,60]. In this study, the Cu-CDNEs with positive charges and peroxidase-like activity were rational designed to against the antibiotic resistance through involving EDA·2HCl to remodel the surface characteristic of Cu-CDs. As shown in Fig. 6 A1 and B1, the Cu-CDNEs exhibit excellent antibacterial capacity for Gram-negative bacteria and Gram-positive bacteria like Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus). From Fig. S15 we can see the zeta potential of bacteria is negative (−15.8 mV, −14.4 mV), and the Cu-CDNEs zeta potential is positive (14.9 mV), and easy to occur electrostatic attraction. After mixed with Cu-CDNEs, the zeta potential of E. coli and S. aureus changed to −7.43 mV and −4.87 mV, respectively, which indicates the generation of electrostatic interaction. Thus, the inherent antibacterial activity of Cu-CDNEs should be attributed to their small size and surface positive charges, enabling that can firmly bind bacteria to collapse the structure of bacteria. The Cu-CDNEs-H2O2 possesses superior antibacterial ability than single Cu-CDNEs due to the synergistic effect of ROS and electrostatic action (Fig. 6A1/B1/A4/B4), while the Glu-Cu and Cu-CDs with negligible catalytic activity cannot realize effective antibacterial function (Fig. S16, S17). Based on this finding, the antibacterial properties of Cu-CDNEs at different concentrations were further investigated. As shown in Fig. 6A4/B4/A5/B5, under the Cu-CDNEs concentration of 50 μg mL−1, the bactericidal rates of the Cu-CDNEs-H2O2 group against E. coli and S. aureus were 97.8 % and 97.6 % that significantly higher than the Cu-CDNEs group (71 % for E. coli and 92 % for S. aureus), and the bacterial growth can be almost absolute inhibited when the Cu-CDNEs concentration over 100 μg mL−1. The satisfactory results prove that the unprecedented advantage of designed Cu-CDNEs in antibacterial field based on the synergistic effect of autologous surface positive charge and ROS produced from catalyzing H2O2. In addition, the Cu-CDNEs also has good biocompatibility, and the cell survival rate still can reach 80.3 % when the Cu-CDNEs concentration achieves 500 μg mL−1 that five times higher than required antibacterial concentration (Fig. S18). The insignificant cell-toxicity of Cu-CDNEs provides a powerful guarantee for the low-risk biomedical application.

In summary, we have developed minute-level solid phase synthesis method for obtaining Cu-CDNEs with both outstanding optical properties and peroxidase-like activity just through microwave-assisted pyrolysis precursor for 5 min. The colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode detection platform for H2S was constructed based on the peroxidase-like activity inhibition of Cu-CDNEs by H2S and the competiveness of H2S and TMB to ROS. The established platform has high-sensitivity and high-specificity that can against the interference from biothiols effectively, and convenient scanometric analysis mode was further fabricated for rapid detecting H2S successfully in real water samples. Furthermore, the surface electro positivity and peroxidase-like activity of designed Cu-CDNEs enables perfect broad-spectrum antibacterial capacity, providing an admirable candidate for biomedical applications. The main advantages of this study reflect in the ingenious design and facile synthesis of Cu-CDNEs and suitable applications of that in H2S assay and antibacterial, which may promote a sunward development of the multifunctional nanozymes.

Zihan Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Mingyang Zhou: Methodology, Funding acquisition. Lizhi Zhao: Funding acquisition. Wei Hu: Funding acquisition. Xin Wang: Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Hua Wei: Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Xinxin Tong: Investigation. Changqing Yao: Investigation. Xiaoqing Li: Investigation. Yutong Zhang: Investigation. Jian Liu: Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Shenghong Yang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ZR2022MC153, ZR2023MA089), the Program of New Collegiate 20 Items in Jinan (Nos. 2021GXRC042, 202228031), the Science, Education and Industry Integration Innovation Pilot Project from Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences) (Nos. 2022JBZ02-04, 2024GH25, 2023PY051)

ce:section.ce:para:
